file_name: Barakam and Sarmadi - 2023 - Machine learning based classification of medical images of melanoma using transfer based learning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used transfer learning with deep learning models (InceptionV3 and VGG16) to classify medical images of melanoma into benign and malignant classes. The study utilized a dataset of 10,600 skin lesion images from Kaggle, processed them using Keras and Python, and evaluated the AI models' performance using metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and AUC.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and optimization of transfer learning models (InceptionV3 and VGG16) for classifying melanoma images. It details specific methods (data augmentation, model architecture), a dataset (10,600 images from Kaggle), and reports new empirical results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC metrics) comparing model performance, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Machine Learning Based Classification of Medical Images of Melanoma Using Transfer Based Learning
brief_summary: This study utilized transfer learning to classify melanoma skin lesions into benign and malignant categories using a dataset of 10,000 images from Kaggle. The researchers implemented and optimized two deep learning architectures, InceptionV3 and VGG16, within a Python environment using Keras. Model performance was evaluated across various learning rates and epoch counts, with metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and AUC. The results indicated that the InceptionV3 model outperformed VGG16, achieving an accuracy of 90%, an AUC of 0.9, and a specificity of 97% with a learning rate of 0.001 and 10 epochs. The findings suggest that optimized transfer learning models can effectively support the early and automated diagnosis of melanoma in clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies skin lesions into two binary classes: benign and malignant. The malignant class specifically refers to melanoma. The dataset contained 10,600 images, with half (50%) being malignant (melanoma) and half (50%) being benign.

The study classifies skin lesions into two binary classes: benign and malignant. The benign class serves as the comparator to malignant melanoma. The dataset contained 10,600 images, with half (50%) being benign and half (50%) being malignant.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesion images into benign and malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification of medical images of melanoma into benign and malignant'. The model uses a sigmoid activation function in the final layer to produce a probability score for binary classification (benign vs. malignant). Performance was evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1 score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from Kaggle, cited as Javid (2022), containing skin lesion images for melanoma classification.

use.training: Used for training the InceptionV3 and VGG16 models (90% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing the models (10% of the dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and AUC.

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Im.experiment: Optimization of Learning Rate and Epochs (10 epochs)

optimal_performance_summary.notes: Textual summary in Discussion/Conclusion states 90% accuracy, 82% sensitivity, 97% specificity, 98% precision, and AUC of 0.9 for the optimized InceptionV3 model.

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Im.notes: The paper states that InceptionV3 outperformed VGG16. The optimal configuration for both models was found to be a learning rate of 0.001 and 10 epochs. Training accuracy for InceptionV3 was approximately 0.9 (90%) with a loss of 0.3, while VGG16 had an accuracy of 0.8 (80%) with a loss of 0.4.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models used as base for transfer learning; base model weights were frozen during training.

preprocessing_techniques: Rescaling pixel values to a range of 0 to 1
Rotation up to a maximum of 20% of the original image's total angle

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Pixel values rescaled to a range of 0 to 1 using ImageDataGenerator.
Input data randomly flipped horizontally to create mirror images.
Input data randomly rotated by a maximum of 20% of the original image's total angle.

conclusions: Transfer learning is a viable and effective approach for the automated classification of melanoma images into benign and malignant categories, particularly in clinical settings where large datasets are limited.

The optimized InceptionV3 model outperformed the VGG16 model in classifying melanoma images, demonstrating that InceptionV3's intricate architecture with inception modules is more effective at capturing nuanced features to differentiate between benign and malignant cases.
The optimal hyperparameters for both models were found to be a learning rate of 0.001 and 10 epochs.

The optimized InceptionV3 model achieved a testing accuracy of approximately 90%, a sensitivity of 82%, a specificity of 97%, and an AUC of 0.9, indicating strong performance in differentiating between classes with a low false positive rate.
The developed model has the potential to contribute to the early and automated diagnosis of melanoma in clinical settings, thereby improving patient outcomes and reducing healthcare costs.
important_remarks: The abstract contains a contradictory statement claiming 'an optimized VGG16 model outperformed VGG16', which is logically impossible and likely a typo for InceptionV3; the results and discussion confirm InceptionV3 was the superior model.
The study relies on a single, small public dataset from Kaggle (10,000 images) with no external validation, limiting the generalizability of the findings to broader clinical populations.
The dataset is artificially balanced (50% benign, 50% malignant), which does not reflect the real-world prevalence of melanoma and may lead to overestimated specificity in clinical deployment.
The authors explicitly acknowledge that the model was trained on a small dataset and would need adaptation to a much larger dataset (e.g., 1,000,000 images) for effective clinical prediction.
No statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between InceptionV3 and VGG16.
The input images were resized to 150x150 pixels, which may result in the loss of fine-grained diagnostic features critical for melanoma detection compared to higher-resolution inputs.

The study was conducted by a high school student with an advisor, which may raise questions regarding the rigor of the experimental design and peer review process compared to standard clinical research.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while a train-test split is mentioned, the source of the Kaggle dataset and potential overlaps with pre-training data (ImageNet) are not discussed.
